summary: DHH comments on AMD's decision to de-emphasize megahertz counts in their marketing, arguing this levels the competitive playing field against Intel. He credits AMD's competition with preventing Intel from dictating the pace of PC evolution through corporate strategy alone. Without AMD, new processors would be artificially delayed and expensive. While AMD still plays in Intel's market, removing the megahertz comparison gives them a better tool to compete.
key_points: AMD's competition with Intel prevents monopolistic control over PC processor evolution and pricing
Megahertz counts are a largely meaningless metric that consumers wrongly use as the sole comparison point
AMD dropping megahertz from marketing won't eliminate Intel's advantage but reduces the bias in how processors are compared
Without competitive pressure, incumbents will artificially slow innovation to maximize revenue from existing products
money_quotes: Without AMD nipping Intels heels, corporate strategy would reign supremely in deciding the pace of the PC evolution.
New processors would patiently wait for older models to complete their tour of duty before being introduced.
The state of the art would carry a $1000 price tag.
We've been there, we don't want to go back.
Having the megahertz count disappear from AMD's marketing material will make the rules slightly less biased. It won't be free homeruns, but a better bat.
key_insight: Competition forces innovation and fair pricing, and AMD's move to drop megahertz marketing removes a misleading metric that favored the incumbent.
